title: Dual role of core electrons in electronic friction
abstract: Non-equilibrium energy dissipation in multi-shell swift-ion/matter systems remains a fundamental yet incompletely understood problem, with electronic stopping power $\mathcal{S}_\text{e}$ as a relevant observable for electronic friction. Using real-time time-dependent density functional theory, we perform first-principles calculations of $\mathcal{S}_\text{e}$ for beryllium self-irradiation with explicit treatment of all electrons. Our results reveal a Bragg peak exhibiting a distinct structure which lies beyond the reach of standard semi-empirical models. We attribute its appearance to a dual effect of the presence of core electrons, by which their excitation provides an additional dissipation channel while simultaneously suppressing valence electron excitations. An electron capture process by the projectile's core from the host cores is behind such suppression, rather than Pauli blocking. This dual mechanism contrasts with the shake-up effect reported for water, whereby the inclusion of core electrons enhances valence excitation. Our work provides a new perspective on the effect and importance of core electrons in projectile energy dissipation in matter.
summary: This paper investigates how core electrons influence the energy loss of ions passing through matter. It discovers that core electrons both add a new way for energy to dissipate and reduce the excitation of valence electrons through electron capture. This finding challenges standard semi-empirical models and provides a new perspective on ion-matter interactions.
main_problem: Understanding the role of core electrons in non-equilibrium energy dissipation and electronic stopping power during swift-ion irradiation.
main_result: The study identifies a dual role for core electrons: they provide an additional dissipation channel through excitation while simultaneously suppressing valence electron excitations via an electron capture process.
method: First-principles calculations using real-time time-dependent density functional theory (rt-TDDFT) with explicit treatment of all electrons.
model_or_system: Beryllium self-irradiation, treated as a multi-shell swift-ion/matter system.
key_observables: Electronic stopping power (Se) and the structure of the Bragg peak.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of real-time TDDFT for describing the electron dynamics during the ion-matter interaction.
paper_structure: The paper presents a problem statement regarding energy dissipation, describes the TDDFT methodology, presents results regarding the Bragg peak structure, discusses the dual mechanism of core electrons, and compares findings to existing models like the shake-up effect in water.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
